page: Vultr vbm-32c-755gb-amd is a bare metal server configured with an AMD EPYC 9354P processor built on the x86_64 architecture. This system delivers 64 physical cores and 64 vCPUs with a dedicated allocation, operating at a base clock speed of 3.25 GHz. It features 768.0 GB of DDR5 memory running at 5 GHz, translating to 12.0 GB of RAM per core. Storage is provided by a bundled 960 GB local SSD, and the server includes one complimentary public IPv4 address. No GPU hardware is present on this instance.

Benchmark results indicate strong capabilities across compute and memory subsystems. The single-core CPU performance ranks in the top 10% under stress-ng testing, while multi-core performance falls in the top 25%. The DDR5 memory architecture achieves top-tier status in PassMark memory benchmarks, with read-only small block memory bandwidth reaching 5029.45 GB/sec. Database operations and LLM inference tasks also show high efficiency, with PassMark database operations scoring in the top 10% and text generation for a medium 7B model achieving top-tier performance at 55.29 tokens per second.

Given the bare metal architecture and high memory-to-core ratio, this server offers high resource density and qualitative cost efficiency for workloads requiring dedicated physical hardware without virtualization overhead. The combination of 768 GB of high-speed DDR5 RAM and 64 dedicated AMD EPYC cores makes this server type highly suitable for memory-intensive databases, large-scale Redis deployments, multi-threaded data decompression, and CPU-based machine learning inference.
description: Vultr vbm-32c-755gb-amd is a bare metal server featuring a dedicated AMD EPYC 9354P processor with 64 physical cores and 64 vCPUs running at 3.25 GHz. It is equipped with 768.0 GB of high-speed 5 GHz DDR5 memory, providing 12.0 GB of RAM per core, and includes a 960 GB local SSD. Lacking a GPU, the server relies on its x86_64 CPU architecture, which achieves top-tier single-core performance and strong multi-core benchmark results. The DDR5 memory subsystem delivers exceptional bandwidth, placing it in the top 10% for PassMark memory and database operations. This hardware profile is highly efficient for memory-intensive workloads, including large database deployments, Redis caching layers, and CPU-based LLM inference.
